digest.top_line: TORPEdO: IMPT vs IMRT no HR-QoL benefit in OPSCC at 12 months (phase III, N=205); proton advantage not demonstrated under UK trial conditions.
digest.tldr: Head-neck and oligomets led: TORPEdO phase III null for IMPT vs IMRT challenges proton ROI for OPSCC; EXTEND (PFS HR 0.54, p<0.001) is the largest randomized MDT signal across histologies. SBRT/hypofractionation equivalences stacked in breast (NRG 1005), prostate (HEAT), and kidney (FASTRACK II); HCC EBRT IPD (N=4,913) supports parity with resection.

sites.intro: OPERA post-hoc: W14 DRE+rectoscopy appears safe for W&W selection, with 5-yr OP rates matching W24 assessment.

studies.name: OPERA Trial (5-year rectal preservation)
studies.tldr: OPERA post-hoc: W14 DRE+rectoscopy reliably identifies cCR; 5-year OP rates 75% (Arm A) vs 83% (Arm B), p=0.24.
details: 🔍 Post-hoc analysis of OPERA trial; N=141 evaluable at W14; response assessed by CTRE (DRE + rectoscopy) at W14 vs standard W24 three-modality (DRE, rectoscopy, MRI)
📊 W14 CTRE: 87% (122/141) assessable; 76% achieved good response (cCR+nCR), 24% PR; Arm B 88% vs Arm A 65% (p=0.004)
📊 5-year OP: 75% Arm A vs 83% Arm B (p=0.24); OP similar between cCR and nCR (81% vs 77%)
📐 MRI concordance: TRG1-2 in 98% (80/82) of W14 CR pts
⚠️ Post-hoc analysis of OPERA; W14 assessment not the pre-specified primary decision point; Arm B boosted arm, so response rate difference partly reflects dose effect
🔗 OPERA established contact X-ray boost (Arm B) superiority for OP; this post-hoc asks whether earlier assessment is safe for W&W selection
🔗 nCR at W14 reflects radiation effect, not residual tumor behavior; conclusion is that nCR should not trigger surgery (equivalent 5-yr OP to cCR)
studies.key_figure_caption: W14 good response: Arm B 88% vs Arm A 65% (p=0.004). 5-yr OP: 75% A vs 83% B (p=0.24); cCR vs nCR 81% vs 77%.

verdict.rationale: Post-hoc analysis; W14 assessment was not prespecified primary endpoint. Result is hypothesis-generating for earlier W&W selection timing.
verdict.audience: Locally advanced rectal cancer, post-NAT, organ preservation intent

open_questions: Prospective validation of W14 CTRE as decision point for W&W
Whether nCR at W14 requires W24 re-assessment or can proceed directly to W&W

sites.intro: Two paradigm questions: ENI omission via INRT shows 0% elective nodal failures at 5yr; TORPEdO finds no HR-QoL gain for proton over photon in OPSCC.

studies.name: INRT-AIR & DARTBOARD (ENI omission in HNSCC)
studies.tldr: ENI omission via INRT: 0% solitary elective nodal recurrence at 5 years, 5-yr OS 87% in pooled N=117.
details: 📊 5-yr solitary elective nodal recurrence 0%; 5-yr OS 87%, PFS 74%
🔍 Patient-level pooled analysis, N=117, oropharynx/larynx/hypopharynx, stage I-IVB (excl T1-2N0 larynx), median f/u 3.4 yrs; AI-assisted suspicious LN identification
💊 INRT omits ENI entirely; PET/CT + neck CT required for eligibility
📐 Mean composite MDADI score at 12 months 84.9, no significant decline post-treatment
⚠️ Pooled prospective cohort, not randomised; N=117 limits power for rare events; authors explicitly state RCT required before non-trial implementation
🔗 vs standard definitive CRT with ENI: reduced OAR dose is the claimed benefit, but no toxicity comparison arm reported in source

verdict.rationale: Pooled prospective cohort, N=117, no randomised comparator. Authors state RCT needed before adoption outside trial.
verdict.audience: Locally advanced HNSCC (oropharynx, larynx, hypopharynx), stage I-IVB, fit for d

open_questions: RCT of INRT vs standard ENI: non-inferiority for regional control?
Quantified OAR dose reduction and late toxicity vs ENI arm?

studies.name: TORPEdO
studies.tldr: TORPEdO phase III: no HR-QoL benefit for IMPT vs IMRT at 12 months in OPSCC (N=205).
details: 📊 Co-primary PRO endpoint: UW-QoL physical composite (saliva, taste, chewing, swallowing, appearance, speech) — no differences between arms at 3, 12, or 24 months post RT
🔍 Phase III RCT, N=205, 2:1 IMPT:IMRT; 70/56 Gy in 33 fractions over 6.5 weeks; cisplatin 100mg/m² D1+D22; bilateral neck treatment required
⚠️ Clinician-reported co-primary (CTCAE G3 weight loss or gastrostomy dependence at 12 months) not reported in source slides; only PRO data shown at ESTRO
⚠️ @AmadeoWals: novel UK centers + identical planning constraints likely capped IMPT dosimetric advantage; experienced proton centers still see clinical benefit
🔗 McBride/Riaz/Sherman/Tsai/Mell, Lancet 2026 (PMID 42134353): concurrent publication on proton vs photon in OPC
⚠️ HR-QoL declines at end-of-CRT then recovers; some pts have meaningful deterioration to 2 years; 5-year PRO follow-up ongoing

verdict.rationale: Phase III RCT null for patient-reported QoL co-primary; challenges proton adoption rationale in OPSCC. Clinician-reported co-primary still pending.
verdict.audience: Oropharyngeal SCC requiring bilateral neck concurrent CRT

open_questions: Does IMPT reduce clinician-reported G3 weight loss or gastrostomy dependence (co-primary pending)?
Does center IMPT experience level modify QoL outcomes?
Will 5-year HR-QoL or late toxicity curves diverge?

sites.intro: NRG 1005 closes the concurrent vs sequential boost debate: noninferior oncologic and cosmetic outcomes, shorter overall treatment.

studies.name: NRG/RTOG 1005
studies.tldr: Concurrent boost during WBI noninferior to sequential boost for IBR (HR 1.31, 90% CI 0.84-2.04) with equivalent toxicity and cosmesis, shortening overall treatment.
details: 📊 1° EP (IBR): 7-yr IBR 2.6% concurrent vs 2.2% sequential; HR 1.31 (90% CI 0.84-2.04), p=0.037 for noninferiority (upper bound 2.04 < prespecified margin 2.12)

🔍 Phase III RCT, N=2,255 eligible; higher-risk early breast cancer post-lumpectomy; 278 sites across North America; median f/u 7.3 yrs; concurrent arm 40 Gy/15F + 8 Gy/15F simultaneous integrated boost vs sequential 50 Gy/25F or 42.7 Gy/16F + 12-14 Gy boost
📊 No differences in DFS, DDFS, OS, or regional nodal recurrence (7-yr RNR 1.2%) between arms

📊 Cosmesis (BCTOS at 3yr): concurrent arm noninferior to sequential by both patient-reported (mean change 0.18 vs 0.16) and physician rating (82.4% vs 85.9% excellent/good, p=0.34); central photo review 72.0% vs 64.2% (p=0.11, also noninferior)
📊 G3-4 toxicity: no significant differences between arms (p=0.81); radiation dermatitis, fatigue, and breast pain most common across both
🔍 Population: median age 55 yrs; 96.8% invasive, 52.7% grade 3, 29.6% ER-neg, 61.8% received chemotherapy; 16.3% node-positive

⚠️ Superiority analysis (prespecified secondary, triggered by meeting noninferiority) was not significant; 90% CI used throughout (not 95%), consistent with noninferiority trial convention but worth noting when interpreting HR precision

verdict.rationale: Phase III RCT, prespecified noninferiority endpoint met, 7+ yr f/u, no toxicity or cosmesis tradeoff; concurrent boost shortens treatment duration without compromise.
verdict.audience: Higher-risk early breast cancer, post-lumpectomy, breast conservation

open_questions: Benefit of concurrent boost with hypofractionated schedules beyond 15F or with APBI?
Cosmesis durability beyond 3 years with concurrent approach?

sites.intro: SBRT equivalence consolidates (HEAT interim); PEACE 2 challenges pelvic RT benefit for very high-risk disease staged with conventional imaging.

studies.name: HEAT Trial
studies.tldr: HEAT (interim): SBRT non-inferior to moderate hypofractionation, BF 7% vs 7.4% at 4.25y (p-noninferiority=0.007), localized PCa.
details: 🔍 Phase III non-inferiority RCT; n=156 randomized (interim), 142 analyzed; 82.4% intermediate-risk; median FU 59.7mo; 28% received ADT
💊 AHRT: 36.25Gy/5fx (7.25Gy/fx) + GTV SIB 40Gy; EHRT: 70.2Gy/26fx IMRT (2.7Gy/fx); ADT ≤6mo permitted both arms
📊 Acute G2+ GI toxicity lower with AHRT; late G2+ GI and acute/late G2+ GU comparable between arms (no effect sizes reported in source)
⚠️ Interim analysis only; full accrual goal n=456, n=420 evaluable enrolled; prespecified final primary analysis pending
🔗 First 1:1 AHRT vs EHRT RCT with modern IMRT + ADT; PACE-B, HYPO-RT-PC, NRG-GU005 excluded ADT or used heterogeneous control arms

verdict.rationale: Interim analysis; full n=420 evaluable cohort not yet reported. Non-inferiority met but prespecified final analysis pending.
verdict.audience: Localized low-to-intermediate risk prostate cancer, IPSS <12

open_questions: Final primary analysis with complete n=420 evaluable cohort
Late toxicity durability beyond 5y follow-up
Whether ADT modifies differential efficacy or toxicity between AHRT and EHRT

studies.name: PEACE 2
studies.tldr: PEACE 2 phase III: pelvic RT did not improve cPFS vs prostate-only RT (HR 0.81, p=0.088) in very high-risk localized prostate cancer.

details.text: 🔍 Phase III 4-arm factorial RCT (±pelvic RT × ±cabazitaxel); N=761 pelvic RT comparison
subdetails: Eligibility: ≥2 of Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, PSA ≥20 ng/mL; N0M0 on conventional/Choline PET
ADT 3 yrs; high-dose RT to prostate ± whole pelvis

details: 📊 No improvement in MFS, OS, or PCSS with pelvic RT; no effect sizes reported in source for these endpoints
🔗 vs POP-RT (Lancet 2021): pelvic RT improved bPFS (HR 0.50) and DFS in high-risk CaP; PEACE 2 fails to replicate in very high-risk, conventionally-staged pts
⚠️ Staged with conventional imaging/Choline PET, not PSMA-PET; PSMA era likely reclassifies occult nodal disease, limiting generalizability
⚠️ AEs minimal with modern RT per presenter; no specific G3+ rates reported in source

verdict.rationale: Phase III RCT, 7-yr f/u; primary cPFS not met (p=0.088); no effect on MFS, OS, or PCSS. Null result challenges pelvic RT use in high-risk disease.
verdict.audience: Very high-risk localized prostate cancer (Gleason ≥8/T3-T4/PSA ≥20), N0M0 conv i

open_questions: Does PSMA-PET staging identify a nodal subgroup that benefits from pelvic RT?
Does cabazitaxel modify pelvic RT benefit in 4-arm interaction analysis?
OS signal with longer follow-up beyond 7 years?

sites.intro: N=4,913 IPD cohort makes a data-level case for EBRT inclusion in BCLC-0/A decision algorithms.

studies.name: HCC EBRT multinational IPD cohort
studies.tldr: Largest multinational EBRT cohort (N=4,913 HCC pts): mOS 6.8yr BCLC-0, 4.6yr BCLC-A; outcomes comparable to resection/ablation.
details: 📊 mOS: 6.8yr (95% CI 5.7-8.7) BCLC-0, 4.6yr (95% CI 4.1-5.1) BCLC-A; treatment-naive BCLC-0 mOS not reached (95% CI 8.6-NR), BCLC-A 5.4yr (95% CI 4.5-6.7)
🔍 Systematic review + IPD from multinational cohorts; N=4,913; median f/u 5.0yr; search date Dec 2022; KM + RMST by BCLC stage/treatment status; random-effects Cox modeling
📊 Ablative RT dose and more recent treatment year both independently associated with reduced mortality risk in multivariable models
🔗 Authors conclude OS comparable to resection, thermal ablation, and other ablative locoregional therapies for BCLC-0/A; directly supports EBRT inclusion in BCLC decision algorithm

⚠️ Non-randomised IPD cohort; era effects acknowledged (more recent tx year = better OS, mixing older lower-dose and newer ablative-dose cases); no direct head-to-head comparator arm within study; heterogeneous centers/techniques across multinational institutions

verdict.rationale: Large multinational IPD cohort (N=4,913) with 5yr median f/u supports EBRT parity with ablative locoregional therapies; consistent with recent guideline inclusion trend.
verdict.audience: HCC BCLC-0/A, treatment-naive and experienced, across Child-Pugh A/B/C

open_questions: Randomised trial vs resection or thermal ablation needed to confirm OS equivalence
Optimal dose/fractionation (ablative vs conventional) still to be defined prospectively

sites.intro: EXTEND (N=334) is the largest randomized MDT trial to date; PFS benefit across most histology baskets, breast and kidney inconclusive.

studies.name: EXTEND Trial
studies.tldr: EXTEND: MDT+SOC vs SOC in oligomets, PFS HR 0.54 (0.41-0.72), p<0.001 across all baskets.
details: 📊 1° EP PFS: HR 0.54 (95% CI 0.41-0.72), p<0.001 (per-protocol, all baskets); excluding prostate baskets HR 0.60 (0.40-0.89)

🔍 Phase II RCT, multicenter, N=334 analyzed per protocol (MDT+SOC n=166, SOC n=168); 1-5 mets, 6 baskets (breast, pancreas, kidney, 2 prostate, Other); median f/u 53 months; RT used as MDT for 98% of mets
🔗 vs SABR-COMET (Lancet 2019, N=99) and STOMP (JCO 2018): EXTEND larger, longer f/u, basket-stratified, broader histology; consistent direction of benefit across non-RCC/non-breast histologies

⚠️ Phase II only, not powered for OS; basket-level results heterogeneous (breast + kidney inconclusive), so overall HR may overrepresent responder histologies; no effect sizes reported per basket beyond directionality
🔬 ctDNA detectable at enrollment correlated with shorter PFS/OS; ctDNA clearance at 3mo post-enrollment correlated with improved survival — exploratory, hypothesis-generating
⚠️ MDT-induced systemic immune activation was most pronounced in baskets with PFS superiority — intriguing mechanistic signal but exploratory only

verdict.rationale: Phase II; basket-level results heterogeneous; breast and kidney inconclusive. Warrants histology-specific phase III, not practice change.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic solid tumors, 1-5 mets, multiple histologies, on SOC systemic th

open_questions: Which histologies warrant phase III: breast and kidney still open
Can ctDNA clearance at 3mo select pts most likely to benefit from MDT?
OS benefit and optimal MDT timing/fractionation undefined

sites.intro: FASTRACK II: 84-month f/u confirms SABR durability for inoperable primary RCC; single-arm design limits comparator inference.

studies.name: FASTRACK II (TROG 15.03)
studies.tldr: FASTRACK II: 100% local control at 36, 60, and 84 months with SABR for inoperable primary RCC (N=70, median f/u 62 mo).
details: 📊 1° EP local control (RECIST): 100% at 36, 60, and 84 months; no observed local recurrences or cancer-related deaths
🔍 Non-randomised phase 2, N=70 (1 withdrew pre-tx), 8 sites Australia/Netherlands; median age 77, 70% male; median tumour 46mm; 56% T1b, 34% T1a, 9% T2a, 1% T3a; 1 N1
💊 Single-fraction 26 Gy for ≤4cm; 42 Gy/3fx (48h apart) for >4cm
⚠️ G3 AEs in 10% within 9mo: nausea/vomiting 4%, abdominal/flank/tumour pain 6%, colonic obstruction 3%, diarrhoea 1%; no G4 events or treatment-related deaths
⚠️ Single-arm, no randomised comparator vs partial nephrectomy or thermal ablation; inoperable/high-risk/declined-surgery pts only; local control endpoint not OS or PFS
🔗 Consistent with prior single-arm SABR-RCC data (STAR-TRK, SAFIR cohorts); long f/u here is the differentiator but design limitations remain

verdict.rationale: Single-arm phase 2 in inoperable pts only; no randomised comparator vs partial nephrectomy or ablation. Local control endpoint, not OS.
verdict.audience: Inoperable or surgery-declined primary RCC, tumours ≤10cm, ECOG ≤2, N0-N1, media

open_questions: Randomised comparison vs thermal ablation or partial nephrectomy in operable pts
OS and distant PFS at long-term follow-up in this cohort
Optimal fractionation for T2+ or >4cm tumours

ESTRO
2026
Five-year Results of the OPERA Trial: When and How to
Assess Tumor Response to Guide Rectal Preservation
Background & Methods
o When organ preservation (OP) is the goal evaluating tumor response after
neoadjuvant therapy (NAT) is a critical step in the decision. Achieving OP first
requires obtaining a complete clinical response (cCR)
o In the OPERA trial: tumour response was assessed at week 24 (W24) using a
three-modality approach : digital rectal examination (DRE), rectoscopy, and MRI.
This triad determined CR and eligibility for OP
• This post-hoc analysis evaluates earlier decision (W14) and clinical tumor response
evaluation (CTRE) through DRE and rectoscopy.
o At week 14: CTRE defined CR, nCR (near complete response) and PR (partial
response) "good" clinical response (CR) included cCR+nCR. MRI used TRG. Good
responders were considered for OP or reassessed at week 24. CTRE correlated with
relapse and 5-year OP rates.
OncoAlert360-
Oncology
For Colleagues
By Colleagues
Syrine Ben Dhia
Centre Antoine Lacassagne
15-19 MAY 2026
STOCKHOLM, SWEDEN
ESTRO
2026

ESTRO
2026
0000g
Five-year Results of the OPERA Trial: When and How to
Assess Tumor Response to Guide Rectal Preservation
Key Results
• At week 14: 122/141 patients (87%) had CTRE: 76% achieved a good response CR
(c+nCR) and 24% PR
• Arm B: 88% vs Arm A 65% (p=0.004)
• MRI in CR patients showed TRG1-2 in 98% (80/82)
• At 5 years OP was similar between arms (75% A vs 83% B, p=0.24) and between
cCR and nCR (81% vs 77%)
→ Fuse I OPERA TRAL:

Five-year Results of the OPERA Trial: When and How to

Conclusion & Take-Home Message
15-19 MAY 2026
STOCKHOLM, SWEDEN
ESTRO
2026
v Post-hoc analysis from OPERA trial: at week 14(3months - 1 month after NAT
end) :
It is possible using clinical exam (DRE) + Recoscopy (with MRI) to reliably assess
Complete Response and to propose (safely) a W&W strategy.
/ CR at week 14 is strongly concordant with MRI.
The prognostic value of cCR and nCR is similar for OP
V nCR reflects radiation side effects, and should not justify radical surgery.

ESTRO
2026
Results of a Randomized Non-Inferiority Trial of
Hypofractionation via Extended versus
Accelerated Therapy (HEAT) for Prostate Cancer
Background & Methods
Background
• AHRT/SBRT (5 fx) is increasingly used in localized PCa
• Randomized phase Ill data on AHRT remains limited
• Limited IMRT use (HYPO-RT-PC*2)
• Heterogeneous control arms (PACE-B,34 NRG-GU005°)
• No ADT allowed (HYPO-RT-PC,12 PACE-B,3-4 NRG-GU005°)
• No known trial compares AHRT & EHRT 1:1 with modern techniques + ADT
Methods
• HEAT: International phase Ill randomized non-inferiority study
• AHRT: 36.25 Gy in 5 fractions, GTV SIB to 40 Gy
• EHRT: 70.2 Gy in 26 fractions using IMRT in all patients
• Localized low-intermediate risk PCa; ADT allowed in both arms (≤6 months)
• Primary endpoint: Biochemical failure (Phoenix definition)
• Non-inferiority margin = 12%
• Interim analysis presented
15-19 MAY 2026
STOCKHOLM, SWEDEN
'ESTRO
2026

Cohort
• n = 156 randomized, n = 142 analyzed
• 82.4% intermediate-risk
• Median FU = 59.7 months
• 28% received ADT
Biochemical Control
• AHRT non-inferior to EHRT for BF
• 7% vS. 7.4%, Pron-inferiority = 0.007 at 4.25y
Toxicity (Clinician-Reported)
• Acute G2+ GI toxicity lower with AHRT
• No significant difference in late G2+ GI
& acute/late G2+ GU toxicities

BI. Late Grade 27 C

ESTRO
2026
Results of a Randomized Non-Inferiority Trial of
Hypofractionation via Extended versus
Accelerated Therapy (HEAT) for Prostate Cancer
Conclusion & Take-Home Message
Key Takeaways
• HEAT: First randomized phase Ill trial purely comparing AHRT & EHRT 1:1
using modern techniques + ADT
• AHRT is non-inferior to EHRT for biochemical control
• Improved acute GI toxicity profile with AHRT
• Comparable late GI & acute/late GU toxicities
Clinical Implication
•AHRT represents an effective and well-tolerated treatment option for
appropriately selected patients with localized low- to intermediate-risk PCa

Conclusions
• HEAT: First randomized phase III trial comparing AHRT & EHRT 1:1
using modern techniques & ADT while including GG3 disease
• AHRT non-inferior to EHRT for biochemical control
• 1 acute Gl toxicity with comparable late GI/GU toxicities & PROs
• Clinical Implication: AHRT is comparably effective and well-
tolerated in select patients with localized low- to intermediate-risk
PCa, with shorter treatment duration vs. EHRT
• AHRT + ADT could be considered SoC for GG 1-3 disease
• Optimal fractionation for large prostate and high IPSS still unclear

For longitudinal HR-QoL in patients treated with IMPT and IMRT:

• Follow-up of HR-QoL ongoing up to 5 years post CRT

ProstatE cAncer
GETUG
unicancer
PEACE 2: Specific conclusion on pelvic RT
• Pelvic radiotherapy leads to a modest non-significant trend in improving cPFS
in very high-risk prostate cancer patients treated with high dose radiotherapy
and long-term hormonal therapy
• Side effects are minimal with modern radiotherapy techniques, but there is
no effect of pelvic radiotherapy on clinically meaningful endpoints (MFS, OS,
PCSS)
• Based on these results, pelvic radiotherapy cannot be considered a standard
of care in the treatment of prostate cancer patients, even at high risk of nodal
extension but no detectable disease on imaging.

Omission of elective nodal irradiation
in HNSCC: long-term results and
patient-level pooled analysis from 2
prospective trials
Sympascho Young, Dominic Moon, Chien-Yi Liao, Jing
Wang, Liyuan Chen, Mu-Han Lin, Randall Hughes, Chul
Ahn, Vladimir Avkshtol, David Sher
OncoAlert360
Oncology For Colleagues
By Colleagues
Sympascho Young
@DrSymYoung

ESTRO
2026
Omission of elective nodal irradiation in HNSCC
15-19 MAY 2026
STOCKHOLM, SWEDEN
'ESTRO
2026
Background & Methods
• Definitive chemoradiotherapy for HNSCC carries significant long-term
toxicities, with elective nodal irradiation (ENI) as a major contributor of
dose to OARs
• Involved nodal radiotherapy (INRT) is a novel approach that omits ENI,
with potential to reduce RT-induced toxicities and immunosuppression
• We conducted a patient-level pooled analysis from 2 prospective trials
studying INRT: INRT-AIR and DARTBOARD
• The novel INRT approach assisted by an artificial-intelligence model to
identify suspicious lymph nodes
• Eligibility: HNSCC of oropharynx, larynx, and hypopharynx; stage I-IVB
(excluding T1-2N0 larynx); completed PET/CT and neck CT
OncoAlert360*
Oncology
For Colleagues.
By Colleagues
Sympascho Young
@DrSymYoung

ESTRO
2026
Omission of elective nodal irradiation in HNSCC
15-19 MAY 2026
STOCKHOLM, SWEDEN
'ESTRO
2026
Key Results
• 117 patients with median follow-up of 3.4 years
• 5-year risk of solitary elective nodal recurrence was 0%
• 3-year cumulative incidence of local recurrence, regional recurrence,
and distant metastasis were 9.5%, 4.3%, and 11% respectively
• 5-year overall survival and progression-free survival was 87% and
74% respectively
• Mean composite MDADI score at 12 months was 84.9, with no
significant decline after treatment
OncoAlert360*
Oncology
For Colleagues
By Colleagues
Sympascho Young
@DrSymYoung

ESTRO
2026
Omission of elective nodal irradiation in HNSCC
15-19 MAY 2026
STOCKHOLM, SWEDEN
'ESTRO
2026
Conclusion & Take-Home Message
• These long-term results suggest that omission of ENI with our INRT
approach is oncologically safe, with minimal risk of solitary elective
nodal recurrence
• Randomized evidence is required to support this promising paradigm
before implementation in a non-trial setting

papers.title: Concurrent Versus Sequential Radiation Dose Escalation to the Surgical Cavity for Conservative Treatment of High-Risk Early Breast Cancer: NRG/RTOG 1005 Phase III Trial.
papers.journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology
papers.abstract:
PURPOSE: For patients with breast cancer undergoing breast conservation, escalating the dose (boost) of radiation to the lumpectomy cavity after whole-breast irradiation (WBI) reduces ipsilateral breast recurrence (IBR) but extends treatment duration. This phase III trial investigated whether boost delivery during WBI versus after WBI provides noninferior IBR and preserves cosmetic appearance.

METHODS: NRG/RTOG 1005 randomly assigned patients at higher risk for IBR after lumpectomy and axillary surgery to either a sequential boost of 12 Gy in six fractions(F) or 14 Gy in 7F after WBI of 50 Gy in 25F or 42.7 Gy in 16F (sequential arm) or a concurrent boost of 8 Gy in 15F of 0.53 Gy per day with WBI of 40 Gy in 15F (concurrent arm) using 3-dimensional conformal radiation therapy (RT) or intensity-modulated RT. Based on 1.59% 5-year IBR for the sequential arm, defining the noninferiority margin as a hazard ratio upper limit on the 90% CI of 2.12, 2,312 patients provide 80% power for noninferiority of IBR as first recurrence for the concurrent arm. Secondary end points included disease-free survival and overall survival, adverse events (AEs), and cosmetic outcomes.

RESULTS: Between May 24, 2011, and June 20, 2014, 2,354 patients were randomly assigned, with 2,255 eligible for analysis (sequential arm, n = 1,118; concurrent arm, n = 1,137). With median follow-up of 7.3 years, there were 56 IBR events; 5- and 7-year IBR were 2.1% and 2.2% on the sequential arm and 1.9% and 2.6% on the concurrent arm, respectively. The noninferiority criterion was met: HR (90% CI): 1.31 (0.84 to 2.04), P = .037. No differences were observed in AEs, cosmetic outcomes, or survival between arms.

CONCLUSION: Concurrent boost during WBI results in noninferior IBR compared with sequential boost without worsening toxicity or cosmetic outcomes and reduces overall treatment time.
papers.note: Vicini FA, Winter K, Freedman GM, Arthur DW, Rosenstein BS, Bentzen SM, Li XA, Halyard MY, Woodward WA, Bleicher RJ, Taghian A, Lyons J, Tomberlin JK, Seaward SA, Cheston SB, Hoover AC, Anderson BM, Perera FE, Poppe MM, Petersen IA, Jhawar S, Hijal T, Moughan J, Movsas B, White JR. Concurrent Versus Sequential Radiation Dose Escalation to the Surgical Cavity for Conservative Treatment of High-Risk Early Breast Cancer: NRG/RTOG 1005 Phase III Trial. J Clin Oncol. 2026 May 11:JCO2502465. ; PMCID: PMC13166090.

papers.title: Overall Survival Among Patients With Hepatocellular Carcinoma Treated With External Beam Radiation Therapy: Individual Patient Data Outcomes From a Multinational Cohort.
papers.journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology
papers.abstract:
PURPOSE: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) has gained delayed acceptance as a recommended first-line treatment modality for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), given limited evidence that it improves overall survival (OS). We analyzed individual patient data (IPD) from an international cohort to assess OS among patients with HCC treated with EBRT.

METHODS: We performed a systematic review of publications that assessed EBRT, met prespecified technical standards for HCC, and reported OS (search date December 15, 2022). Corresponding authors were invited to submit IPD for the study. We performed Kaplan-Meier survival analyses to determine OS and restricted mean survival time (RMST) stratified by Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) stage and treatment status (ie, treatment-naïve and experienced). We performed random effects Cox proportional hazards modeling to assess clinical characteristics associated with OS.

RESULTS: Data were provided on 4,913 patients treated with EBRT with a median follow-up time of 5.0 years. The median OS was 6.8 years (95% CI, 5.7 to 8.7) for BCLC-0 and 4.6 years (95% CI, 4.1 to 5.1) for BCLC-A. Among treatment-naïve patients, the median OS was not reached (95% CI, 8.6 to not reached) for BCLC-0 and was 5.4 years (95% CI, 4.5 to 6.7) for BCLC-A. In multivariable models, more advanced BCLC stage, higher tumor burden, worse performance status, and Child-Pugh class B or C were associated with a higher risk of mortality. Ablative radiation dose and more recent year of treatment were associated with a reduced risk of death.

CONCLUSION: To our knowledge, this study represents the largest multinational cohort of patients with HCC treated with EBRT. OS outcomes with EBRT for very early- and early-stage HCC appear to be comparable with resection, thermal ablation, and other ablative locoregional therapies. These data support the inclusion of EBRT in the BCLC HCC clinical decision-making process.
papers.note: Moon AM, Yanagihara TK, Dawson LA, Yu JI, Lawrence TS, Kim TH, Yan M, Iwata H, Nabavizadeh N, Apisarnthanarax S, Dunne EM, Lock MI, Chuong MD, Chiang CL, Scorsetti M, Katoh N, Sioshansi S, Numata K, Liu HY, Iwamoto H, Wakatsuki M, Chen Y, Pollom EL, Gkika E, Jabbour SK, Munoz-Schuffenegger P, Dutta D, Hajj C, Ueno M, Hallemeier CL, Feldman AM, Méndèz Romero A, Tan X, Molla M, Tepper JE, Torres F, Reig M; EBRT Collaboration Group. Overall Survival Among Patients With Hepatocellular Carcinoma Treated With External Beam Radiation Therapy: Individual Patient Data Outcomes From a Multinational Cohort. J Clin Oncol. 2026 May 15:JCO2502399.

papers.title: Addition of Metastasis-Directed Therapy to Standard of Care for Oligometastatic Solid Tumors: Primary Analysis of All Tumor-Histology Baskets of the Phase II Randomized EXTEND Trial.
papers.journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology
papers.abstract:
PURPOSE: We tested the hypothesis that adding metastasis-directed therapy (MDT) to standard of care (SOC) systemic therapy improves progression-free survival (PFS) among patients with oligometastatic disease.

METHODS: EXTEND was a multicenter randomized phase II trial. Patients with 1-5 metastases were randomized to MDT+SOC vs SOC in 1 of 6 baskets (breast, pancreas, kidney, two prostate baskets, and an "Other" basket) with basket-specific stratification and powering. PFS, the primary endpoint, was pre-specified in the per-protocol set within each basket, across all baskets, and across all baskets excluding the prostate baskets. Exploratory endpoints included circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) and immune profiling.

RESULTS: From 2018 through 2023, 521 patients were screened, 350 were randomized, and 334 were analyzed per protocol (MDT+SOC, n=166; SOC, n=168). Radiotherapy was used as MDT for 98% of metastases (370/379). Overall, after median follow-up of 53 months, PFS was improved with MDT+SOC (HR 0.54, 95% CI 0.41 to 0.72, p < 0.001). Similarly, PFS was improved when excluding the prostate baskets (HR, 0.60; 95% CI: 0.40 to 0.89). Within each basket, PFS superiority was identified for the pancreas, prostate, and "Other" baskets, whereas the breast and kidney baskets were inconclusive. At enrollment, detectable ctDNA correlated with shorter PFS and survival; by contrast, ctDNA clearance 3-months post-enrollment correlated with improved survival. MDT+SOC-induced systemic immune activation was most pronounced among baskets demonstrating PFS superiority.

CONCLUSION: The phase II EXTEND trial supports the addition of MDT to SOC for oligometastatic disease. Histology-specific efficacy signals were identified for phase III testing. Translational insights suggest the potential for optimizing the definition of oligometastasis using ctDNA, and point to systemic immune responses as a possible mechanism of benefit from MDT.
papers.note: Sherry AD, Haymaker C, Wang S, Liu S, Bathala TK, Medina-Rosales MN, Seo A, Hara K, Reddy J, Chun SG, Mayo LL, Walker G, Pant S, Zhao D, Kovitz CA, Ramirez D, Ha CS, Smith BD, Gomez D, Cohen L, Koong AC, Reuben A, Tannir N, Corn PG, Tran PT, Siddiqui BA, Subudhi SK, Msaouel P, Ludmir EB, Tang C. Addition of Metastasis-Directed Therapy to Standard of Care for Oligometastatic Solid Tumors: Primary Analysis of All Tumor-Histology Baskets of the Phase II Randomized EXTEND Trial. J Clin Oncol. 2026 May 16:101200JCO2502856.

papers.title: Long-term outcomes of stereotactic ablative body radiotherapy for primary kidney cancer (TROG 15.03 FASTRACK II): a multicentre, non-randomised, phase 2 study.
papers.abstract:
BACKGROUND: Stereotactic ablative body radiotherapy (SABR) is an emerging, non-invasive alternative for primary renal cell carcinoma. We aimed to provide the final long-term trial outcomes of TransTasman Radiation Oncology Group (TROG) 15.03 FASTRACK II, the first phase 2 trial investigating SABR for primary renal cell carcinoma to our knowledge.

METHODS: FASTRACK II was a non-randomised, phase 2 study conducted in eight hospitals in Australia and the Netherlands by TROG and the Australian and New Zealand Urogenital and Prostate (ANZUP) Cancer Trials Group. Here, we report the final pre-planned follow-up results. Adult patients (aged ≥18 years) with histologically confirmed primary renal cell carcinoma, who were medically inoperable, high risk, or declined surgery, had an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of 2 or less, had tumours 10 cm or less in size, and had N0-N1 disease were included. Patients underwent either a single fraction SABR of 26 Gy for tumours 4 cm or less in maximum diameter, or 42 Gy in three fractions delivered 48 h apart for tumours more than 4 cm in maximum diameter. The primary outcome was freedom from local progression to assess local control after SABR evaluated with the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumours. The primary endpoint and safety were evaluated in the intention-to-treat population. A patient representative was involved in the study design and conduct. The trial was registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT02613819) and is closed to enrolment.

FINDINGS: Between July 28, 2016, and Feb 27, 2020, 71 patients were enrolled and one withdrew consent before treatment. Median follow-up was 62 months (IQR 60-72), median age was 77 years (70-82). 49 (70%) of 70 patients were male and 21 (30%) were female. Race and ethnicity data were not collected. The median tumour size was 46 mm (37-55), with 24 (34%) patients with T1a disease, 39 (56%) with T1b disease, six (9%) with T2a disease, and one (1%) with T3a disease. One patient (1%) had nodal involvement (N1). SABR resulted in 100% local control at 36 months, 60 months, and 84 months. Seven (10%) patients had at least one grade 3 adverse event within 9 months of SABR that was designated possibly, probably, or definitely related to treatment: nausea and vomiting (three [4%] events); abdominal, flank, or tumour pain (four [6%]); colonic obstruction (two [3%]); and diarrhoea (one [1%]). No new long-term safety signals, grade 4 events, or treatment-related deaths were noted.

INTERPRETATION: Long-term follow-up supports the safety and local control of SABR for non-surgical patients with renal cell carcinoma, with no observed local recurrences or cancer-related deaths in this cohort, which had predominantly T1b disease or higher.

FUNDING: The Cancer Australia Priority-driven Collaborative Cancer Research Scheme and Varian.
papers.note: Siva S, Pryor D, Martin J, Hardcastle N, Moon D, Kron T, Higgs B, Foroudi F, Ruben J, Sridharan S, Montgomery R, Davey R, Lin C, Shaw M, Lawrentschuk N, Appu S, Vanneste BGL, Hofman MS, Murphy DG, De Abreu Lourenco R, Mancuso P, Brook NR, Raman A, Wong LM, Sidhom M, Wood S, Ali M, Bressel M. Long-term outcomes of stereotactic ablative body radiotherapy for primary kidney cancer (TROG 15.03 FASTRACK II): a multicentre, non-randomised, phase 2 study. Lancet Oncol. 2026 May 17:S1470-2045(26)00091-4.
